skill.name: css-development-refactor
skill.description: Refactors inline styles and repeated utility classes into semantic CSS component patterns with dark mode support and test guidance.
skill.summary: Convert scattered CSS into reusable semantic component classes while preserving visual behavior.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, weak-crypto, and network-reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are Markdown examples and normal CSS refactoring instructions. No malicious intent, exfiltration, prompt injection, or executable bundled script was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: External Command Guidance Is Benign
low_findings.description: The reported command patterns are Markdown examples that recommend searching with grep and running npm test during CSS refactoring. These are expected developer actions and do not include shell interpolation, hidden downloads, persistence, or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are fenced Markdown examples or explicit test commands. The context shows ordinary project-local validation, not dynamic command execution or attacker-controlled input.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Access Is Expected For Refactoring
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to read component and CSS files, edit markup, and add tests. This is necessary for a CSS refactoring workflow and no sensitive file targets or broad destructive operations are requested.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are limited to project CSS, components, and tests. There is no evidence of credential access, deletion, or unauthorized file collection.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-cryptography locations occur in descriptive prose about CSS styles and documentation. No cryptographic API, hash function, encryption routine, or security-sensitive algorithm appears in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines discuss CSS refactoring and documentation content. The file contains no code invoking DES, MD5, SHA1, or any cryptographic primitive.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Matches Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The network-reconnaissance alerts map to normal prose and CSS documentation examples, not network tooling. No instruction asks the agent to scan hosts, open sockets, enumerate services, or contact external endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context around the lines concerns creating CSS classes and summarizing maintainability. No network commands or external targets are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project-Local Shell Commands In Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples for grep and npm test. These commands are common for frontend refactoring, but agents should still inspect commands before execution in an untrusted project.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are simple and project-local, but any command execution in a community skill deserves user awareness.

content.value_statement: Scattered inline styles and repeated utility classes make frontend code hard to maintain. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through semantic CSS extraction with dark mode and tests.
actual_capabilities: Identifies inline styles and repeated utility class combinations in frontend files.
Guides extraction of repeated patterns into semantic CSS component classes.
Encourages reuse of existing component classes before creating new styles.
Adds dark mode variants to new or refactored CSS classes.
Updates markup to use semantic class names while preserving extra classes.
Recommends static CSS and component rendering tests for refactored styles.
limitations: Does not include an executable refactoring script or automated parser.
Requires the agent to inspect the project structure before choosing file paths.
Visual equivalence depends on available tests, screenshots, or manual review.
Tailwind @apply guidance may need adjustment for projects without Tailwind.

use_cases.title: Consolidate Repeated Utility Classes
use_cases.description: Turn repeated button, card, badge, or panel utility combinations into named component classes.

use_cases.title: Migrate Inline Styles Safely
use_cases.description: Move inline styling from components into CSS classes while keeping current layout and behavior.

use_cases.title: Add Dark Mode During Cleanup
use_cases.description: Refactor existing component styles and add matching dark mode variants in the same pass.

prompt_templates.title: Find CSS Refactor Targets
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to inspect my frontend files for inline styles and repeated utility class groups. Report the best refactor targets first.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Semantic Classes
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor the repeated utility classes in these components into semantic classes in the project CSS file. Preserve existing behavior and class-specific overrides.

prompt_templates.title: Add Dark Mode Variants
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve a refactor with theme support
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add dark mode variants while extracting semantic CSS classes. Check backgrounds, text colors, borders, and interactive states.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor And Validate
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor scattered CSS into semantic component patterns, update markup, add focused tests, run available validation, and summarize changed files and behavior checks.

output_examples.input: A component file contains many buttons with the same long utility class list.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends one semantic button class, updates each button to use it, and records the replacement count.

output_examples.input: A card component uses inline background, padding, border, and shadow styles.
output_examples.output: The skill moves those styles into a named card class, adds dark mode equivalents, and suggests a rendering test.

output_examples.input: A project has existing component classes that overlap with the new pattern.
output_examples.output: The skill asks the agent to reuse or extend existing classes before adding new CSS.

best_practices: Start with a pattern inventory and refactor the highest-frequency duplication first.
Reuse existing component classes before adding new semantic class names.
Run available tests and inspect light and dark modes after each meaningful change.
anti_patterns: Do not rename classes without checking current markup, variants, and tests.
Do not remove one-off utility classes that express local layout differences.
Do not claim visual behavior is unchanged without validation evidence.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically rewrite CSS?
faq.answer: No. It provides a structured workflow for an agent to inspect files, edit styles, and verify the result.

faq.question: Does it require Tailwind CSS?
faq.answer: The examples use Tailwind @apply, but the semantic refactoring approach can be adapted to regular CSS.

faq.question: Can it add dark mode support?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow explicitly checks backgrounds, text colors, borders, and interactive states for dark mode variants.

faq.question: Will it preserve existing behavior?
faq.answer: That is the goal. The skill instructs the agent to run tests and verify visual output after refactoring.

faq.question: What files does it usually change?
faq.answer: It usually changes component markup, shared CSS files, and focused tests related to the refactored components.

faq.question: Is this safe for large design systems?
faq.answer: Yes, when used incrementally. Start with repeated patterns, keep changes small, and validate each component group.
